steps.title: The Bb4 Pin
steps.text:
The **Nimzo-Indian Defense** is one of the deepest, most respected openings in all of chess. Named after **Aron Nimzowitsch** — the father of *hypermodern chess* and author of *My System* — it's a strategic masterpiece based on one bold idea: **pin the c3 knight and threaten to ruin White's pawn structure**.

## How you reach it

The defining sequence is {{d4}} **{{Nf6}}** {{c4}} **{{e6}}** {{Nc3}} **{{Bb4}}**. Black's bishop on b4 pins the c3 knight to the king (well, *pins* it metaphorically — actually it's pinned to nothing, but **...Bxc3+** would double White's pawns).

## What each side wants

- **White's plan:** avoid the doubled pawns (4.Qc2, 4.a3) or accept them in exchange for the bishop pair (4.e3, 4.f3). Then exploit the central majority and the long-diagonal bishop.
- **Black's plan:** trade the bishop on b4 for the c3 knight at the right moment, leaving White with **doubled c-pawns**. Then blockade those pawns and exploit the structural weakness.
- **Pawn structure:** the *Nimzo doubled c-pawns* (c4 and c3) — a long-term weakness Black plays against for 30+ moves.

## The eternal trade-off

White gets the **bishop pair**; Black gets a **damaged enemy structure**. Whose advantage is greater? It depends on whether the position opens (bishops shine) or stays closed (structure wins). That's the heart of every Nimzo-Indian middlegame.

> The Nimzo isn't about checkmate. It's about pawn structures, piece quality, and 50-move plans.

steps.title: Two Main Lines
steps.text:
On move 4, White has to choose how to handle the pin. Each choice creates a completely different middlegame.

## The four major White responses

- **4.Qc2** — *Capablanca Variation*. White avoids doubled pawns by recapturing with the queen. Modern, popular, slightly more flexible.
- **4.e3** — *Rubinstein System*. The most flexible. White prepares **Nf3, Bd3, 0-0**, sometimes **a3** to chase the bishop. Allows ...Bxc3+ but compensates with the bishop pair.
- **4.f3** — *Kmoch / Sämisch*. Aggressive: White prepares **e4** and a kingside attack, accepting structural damage for activity.
- **4.a3** — *Sämisch / Hübner*. White challenges immediately: bishop must trade or retreat. Almost forces Bxc3+, and White gets the bishop pair plus the open b-file.

## Black's main responses to each line

- After **4.Qc2**: Black plays **...d5** or **...c5** with active piece play.
- After **4.e3**: Black has **...c5**, **...d5**, **...0-0**, **...d5+...c5** (Hübner Variation).
- After **4.f3**: Black plays **...d5** to challenge before White's e4 push lands.
- After **4.a3**: **...Bxc3+ 5.bxc3** is forced, and the strategic battle around White's doubled pawns begins.

## A model main line

{{d4}} {{Nf6}} {{c4}} {{e6}} {{Nc3}} {{Bb4}} **4.e3** **0-0** **5.Bd3** **d5** **6.Nf3** **c5** **7.0-0** **dxc4** **8.Bxc4** **Nbd7** — the *Karpov Variation*, balanced and strategic.

> Whichever line White picks, Black gets a position where understanding beats memorization.

steps.title: Strategic Depth
steps.text:
The Nimzo-Indian is famous for one thing above all: it's **the** opening where understanding pawn structures matters more than calculating tactics.

## The four classic Nimzo pawn structures

- **Doubled c-pawns** (after ...Bxc3+ bxc3) — White has c4-c3, Black plays against c3.
- **Hübner / Hanging Pawns** — Black's d-pawn and c-pawn stand isolated together, both attackable, both flexible.
- **Isolated Queen Pawn (IQP)** — sometimes White accepts an isolated d-pawn for piece activity.
- **Karlsbad-style** — symmetric pawn chains where small breaks decide the game.

## The Nimzo-Indian thinking process

- Is the position **opening up**? Then the bishop pair becomes valuable.
- Is the position **staying closed**? Then doubled pawns and weak squares decide.
- Where does Black **blockade**? Usually with a knight on c4 or e4 — squares the doubled pawns can't defend.
- Where does White **break**? With **e3-e4** or **c4-c5** to liberate the bishops.

## The legacy

World champions **Mikhail Botvinnik**, **Tigran Petrosian**, **Anatoly Karpov**, and **Vladimir Kramnik** all played the Nimzo as their main weapon. Karpov specifically used it to win the world championship — and to *keep* it for over a decade.

> The Nimzo-Indian is positional chess in its purest form. Once you understand it, every other opening becomes easier.
